message:
perf(indexer): use smaller commit intervals before the first durable Tantivy commit during a rebuild

`rebuild_tantivy_from_db` previously held all in-memory writes for
either 10,000 conversations / 200,000 messages / 512 MiB before
issuing the very first commit to the new Tantivy index.  On large
corpora that meant the entire rebuild's exposure to crash / OOM /
SIGTERM was concentrated in the first checkpoint — if it died at
9,999 conversations the whole rebuild restarted from zero on the
next run.

This change separates "initial" commit thresholds from "steady-state"
thresholds:

  CASS_TANTIVY_REBUILD_INITIAL_COMMIT_EVERY_CONVERSATIONS  → 2_048
  CASS_TANTIVY_REBUILD_INITIAL_COMMIT_EVERY_MESSAGES       → 50_000
  CASS_TANTIVY_REBUILD_INITIAL_COMMIT_EVERY_MESSAGE_BYTES  → 128 MiB

and applies them only while `rebuild_state.committed_offset == 0`.
Once a durable commit lands, the existing steady-state thresholds
take over (saturating performance benefit of large batches), and
the new helper `lexical_rebuild_commit_intervals_for_state()`
re-evaluates them on every commit so the transition is automatic
and observable in tests.

Each initial threshold is `min()`-clamped against its corresponding
steady-state threshold so a user who tunes the steady values down
below the initial ones doesn't accidentally wind up with a larger
initial commit interval than steady.

Two unit tests verify both states:
  - `…intervals_start_smaller_before_first_durable_commit` confirms
    the 2_048 / 50_000 / 128 MiB defaults apply when
    `committed_offset == 0`.
  - `…intervals_return_to_steady_state_after_first_commit` confirms
    the 10_000 / 200_000 / 512 MiB steady defaults apply once
    `committed_offset > 0`.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
